title: M-010 per-key lock: RwLock read/write split unblocks hot-key pipelined writes
content: phase12 fast-path. The KVStore per-key lock (audit M-010) existed to isolate MULTI/EXEC from plain non-transactional writers to the same keys. It was a per-shard tokio::Mutex (1024 shards); every KV write did lock_key(key).await. Under pipelined single-hot-key load (redis-benchmark -P16, one key) all writes serialized on ONE async mutex -> SET/INCR capped ~130k rps while lock-free GET hit 833k. Key realization: the M-010 lock only needs to exclude plain writers FROM an EXEC, NOT from each other — two plain SET k are already serialized by the KV shard's parking_lot data RwLock, which is the only thing that mutates state. So it's a reader/writer problem, not mutual exclusion. Fix: change KeyLockManager shards from Mutex<()> to tokio::sync::RwLock<()>. Plain writers call read_key -> read_owned() (shared, don't serialize); EXEC calls write_keys -> write_owned() over its key set (exclusive, still excludes all plain writers). tokio RwLock is write-preferring so a queued EXEC orders new plain writers after it (no starvation, correct ordering both directions). Result: RESP3 SET 130k->757k (5.8x), INCR 134k->458k; SynapRPC SET 81k->330k, INCR 78k->470k; even -P1 improved (SynapRPC SET 88k->170k — RwLock read is cheaper than Mutex even uncontended). Sites: read_key at store.rs set/incr/delete/getset; write_keys at transaction.rs exec. The M-010 durability test (plain_write_is_isolated_from_held_key_lock) had to switch from lock_key to write_keys to simulate EXEC holding the write side. Lesson: before making a lock finer-grained, check whether it even needs to be mutual-exclusion or just one-directional exclusion — a RwLock often applies.
